speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, interview with Chaim Engel. Chaim, would you like to introduce these women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, next to my right is my lovely daughter, Alida Engel. She is recently married, has a lovely husband. Next to her is my wife, my lovely wife, Selma Engel. And next to her is our lovely granddaughter, the daughter of my daughter, and [? Tagan ?] is her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Tagan ?] [? Rosenblatt. ?] Yeah, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that you have a son as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we have a son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who could not be here, today.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he could not be here today.

speaker: interviewer
text: As your three other granddaughters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, two other, three other granddaughters-- two from his daughters, and one of our daughter's younger daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what other names from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kendra is the daughter from my daughter. And Heidi and [? Emmy, ?] my son's, our son's-- his, his daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Liddy. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are sitting here with your parents, who are both survivors. What has it meant for you to be the child of a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's been hard, on many levels. But I think there's also some things that I think that I've gotten as gifts from it. The hard part is, well, just knowing the pain and the grief that they've gone through. And certainly, that affects who I am, and how I think, and how I feel. I think life was more difficult for them, and hence, for me. I know they certainly tried their best and did all they could. But life was hard, all the way around, for everybody. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In every country in which you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was going to say, certainly, emigrating, which is part of being a survivor. It's part of it. I think the hardest was America. I think Holland-- and maybe it has to do something my, how old I was. I was a child. But I have fond memories of Holland, and certainly Israel. I just loved living in Israel. And that was a lot of joy. Coming to this country was very difficult. I was a teenager and was struggling with the language. And we moved into a community that it was hard to blend into. I had a lot of friends. That wasn't hard. But learning the language and competing on that level was very difficult for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that there were positive things, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think that my mother's go-getness-- you know, go for it and make it happen, and get it-- is something that I've learned and has become part of me. I've accomplished a lot-- not, not-- struggling with the language and yet getting a college degree and, and having the children. I was in a marriage that wasn't good-- getting out of that, going to-- I went to grad school. I worked full-time. I raised my children. I created a private practice. I made a lot of money, so they could go to private schools. I paid for everything by myself. I have succeeded in many ways. And I think that that ability to just go for it, you know, if, from both of my parents, is, is part of what is who they are. And it's part of who I am. And now, I'm at a place where there's more peace. I was in therapy for a long, long, long, long time. And, I'm at the other end of a lot of that. But I think it clearly impacted me, and who I was, and who I was as a parent to my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your feelings about being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, again, I think that, that has to do with having grown up in Israel. And I really feel that I've grown up in Israel. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even though I left-- I came when I was five, and I left when I was 11 and 1/2. But those were very fundamental years for me, and very happy years. And in Israel, you were just a Jew. It wasn't an issue. And I did not find that I fit into American Judaism very well. And so I didn't find a home in a Jewish community, not in the one that we belonged to in Westport, or one when I-- that as I was raising the children. Now, I've found a community that, that feels right to me. But it's-- so that-- but I, but I still can't-- I find myself being Jewish, but not religious. And I can't relate to the Torah. When I go to temple, I meditate. I don't read the Torah. It, it really doesn't call to me, at all. And but I feel very Jewish, and maybe more like an Israeli Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you want your daughters to feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In terms of Judaism?

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of Judaism, in terms of being, well, then the grandchildren of survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that they've been very close to my folks, so that a lot of what they've gotten is through me, but through them as well. You know, having-- I was a single parent since before Kendra was born. So my parents were part of raising the children, since we lived so close. And I did not give them a formal Jewish education, because I didn't find a community that had meaning to me. And I was not going to send them someplace that had no meaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't-- I'm not sure I've done it consciously. I've just exposed them to who I am. And I've been a spiritual person. I've been a, a thinking person. I've been a person who's dealt with issues head on. And I think I've given to them through life experiences. And I think-- [? Tagen, ?] were you still home when I started going, walking to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] A little bit, but I didn't go with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. At some point, I did take on the Sabbath and started walking down to shul. And at that time, it was a, it was a place where you could-- very small, and you could go and meditate. And I could kind of go in my jeans and do my own thing. And, and I started doing that. And they could come with me. They didn't have to come, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Kendra that went with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I think [? Tagan ?] did a little bit, too. TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): I did a little. And so I did start creating a Sabbath, because it started having meaning to me from a place that made sense. Yeah, so that I did start doing that. But it's funny, as the shul became really a wonderful place-- and if the kids were little, I would have gone there now-- but as it became a wonderful community place, it lost its sense of spirituality to me. Because it became a social place, and I couldn't go and meditate and kind of find my quiet place. But it is an environment, and that I appreciate, and like, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we ought to ask [? Tagan ?] the question that I asked your mother, about how you feel about being Jewish, knowing your grandparents' experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] It's something that I think about a lot. It's changed a lot throughout my life, in terms of the things that my mom has just said. I grew up not really understanding or knowing very much about Judaism as a religion. As a culture, I knew a lot about it. I mean-- in fact, I, as I got older, started to realize that some of the things I thought of as Jewish were really Dutch things or-- because my grandparents-- you know, the foods and the, some of the words. But I thought about it. Once, I was trying to remember when I first found out about Oma and Opa being survivors. And I think I was in third grade, maybe, when the book and the movie came out. And I remember they were being interviewed on Good Morning America. And I didn't have to go to school. And, but I don't remember before that, although I don't think it was something new. I don't remember being shocked at that time. And I'll kind of just go in chronological order and cover all the similar things. But in terms of-- my mom was talking a little bit about how it, how her parents being survivors impacted her life and her role as a parent. Something that she didn't really get into is how much the fears of the images of the Holocaust impacted who she is, and how-- I mean, she's come a long way from where she was 25 years ago, when I was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're right. TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): And there's depression. There's all these images of ghosts. She would often talk about like these dark images of ghosts. And it was things she tried to keep from us. You know, she wasn't like, oh, this is what I'm thinking. But it's very much a part of her and the fears she had and fears she had about interacting with other people-- things that would come up in the workplace, where, you know, someone who was her boss would, if she was having a conflict with them, it would bring up all these scary things that were much bigger than anything about work. It was something much deeper and very obviously related to things relating to the war. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I just cut in a sec, because you're reminding me, I think, that in all those years of therapy, when I, I never knew what the fear was. And one day, towards, closer towards the end of therapy-- maybe you know, a year towards the end-- it came to me what the fear was. And it was something I was afraid to face for eight or nine years. And the fear was, there was a Gestapo soldier. There was a soldier with a dog, and he was going to get me. That was the fear. That was the fear that had been there all those years, and I never knew what it was. And once I met my fear, life, things started turning around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did the soldier came from? Where did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, it was just an image that was in me, you know, of something to be scared of. And I was always afraid to look to-- I, I knew there was a fear. I was always afraid to look at it. And one day, I felt safe enough in my therapy room-- I had been there enough years-- and safe enough, that I saw what the fear was. And I faced it. And that really changed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the fear is gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it took a while. It took another year to work that through. But it was-- yeah. It was very dramatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] So I was very surprised when I went to Poland-- and then I'll get back to the question of Judaism-- the images that had not directly been put out, but had been there through-- I had studied about the Holocaust when I went to college, and then obviously just, I had spoken to my oma and opa a lot about things that my mom had no idea about. Because you know, the grandchildren always can have the safety and the freedom to ask questions that children don't always have. So I was very interested in that growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I went to Poland, these, there were all these images that came up to me, going when I-- I was on a train entering Poland, and I saw these trees. You know, and it was at dusk. And I just felt terrified, because I had had all these images of Jews in these boxcars going to camps. And I was feeling like, here I am, on a train, going into Poland. And there are these trees. And I had always had these images of people running through the trees, because they had told me when they left the camp, running through trees. And Oma was taking off all the clothes she had on, because she was so hot. And Opa was saying, no, keep them on. Because at night, you'll need them. And these images that were so clear to me and just actually being in Poland, that they were so evident was-- it was shocking to me how strong those images were for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote many things, while you were in Poland. [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] Yes. Would you like to read something?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] Sure. I'll read-- this was something I wrote kind of about what I just said. 'I think of Poland as it was 50 years ago, as does my oma, saying don't go, and don't tell anyone you're Jewish. I comply, thinking all she can remember is months of starvation, sorting through death and clothes, remembering life nourished only by death. In her fears, as she ran with Opa in their few days of terror-stricken freedom, before locking themselves in a hayloft to wait. But the Poland I see out this window is not much different-- tall, withered trees standing so close, it looks as if nothing could slip between them. It looks as if nothing could slip between their bodies, as they lay scattered in ditches, the air of fresh execution still wheezing from their lungs. I can imagine no joy here as I pass boxcars and old, crumbling buildings, strategically placed at the end of each train platform. I can only think of one thing-- images rooted deep in my existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I try to soothe myself by remembering that once, once, this land was covered with Jews-- not their ashes, scattered from ovens, but alive, living, here, in those buildings. And as I stare out this window and see horse-drawn plows, wide open fields of desolation, soldiers in uniform swarming with the casual gait of youth, I wonder what it will be like when I take my first step of this train?' What was it like when you took that first step off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): I was in Warsaw. And it was actually pretty scary. I was--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Right there, Daddy. Right there. Tissues.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's tissues right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] The first day was, was scary and hard. I was alone when I was in Warsaw. I got there. Everything was very gray. The area around the train station was very gray, and these huge buildings that took up a block. And, and I was staying right around the train station that night. And so the first night was hard. But I went out walking, and found a cafe and bumped into a bunch of American basketball players. And things quickly turned around. And, and I felt very exhilarated to be there. And actually, I'll read one short poem that connects to that. So something about-- I had also, at that time, I had been in Israel and then went to Poland. And then as a result of being in Poland, decided to go back to Israel. I was supposed to continue to Holland and see where my mother was born and realized that I needed to go back to Israel. 'I was sitting atop that tree, just soaking up the sun, when the tips of those roots got so thirsty, they just sucked me right up. And I slid down that trunk with such ease to enter the depths of the earth and meet the source of my life.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So going to Poland, really, up until that point-- as my mother said-- we didn't have a Jewish education. I went to public school, where I was until I was in college. I was in public school. I was surrounded by kids of every different ethnicity, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Magnet school. [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] To the magnet schools. And I became very much more interested in African and African-American cultures and went to Brazil when I was in college. And that much-- something I felt much more connected to, in terms of the reality of my life. And I remember in sixth grade, a girl, a friend of mine who was black, said to me, you're going to go to hell, because you don't believe in Jesus. And I had no idea what she was talking about. Like, I didn't know what-- I knew it had to do with me being Jewish, but I didn't know how to respond. I didn't know what she meant, nothing. And I had-- I know that I, I had a lot of friends who were Jewish, but all of them were culturally Jewish. And none of their parents really practiced. And we, we celebrated the high holidays, and I always enjoyed that. But I always kind of had this resistance to being involved in Jewish things, because all the Jewish kids I knew who were religiously Jewish were in the suburbs, were from a very different environment than, than I was. And the first two months I spent in Israel after college, I realized I really wanted to-- I knew so much about where so many other people in the world were from and their histories, and their languages. And, and I finally said, I don't know about my own people. And especially because of my oma and opa being survivors, it's, that has been a very strong force in my life, where I really-- I felt that energy of, I can tackle anything. I can overcome anything. And the blessings of that our family has had-- this blessing of being able to survive, out of all the millions of people, that for some reason, we were able to survive. And that has always been clear to me, that feeling in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first two months that I was in Israel, I got there. And I was like, who, who would want to learn Hebrew? Hebrew's this ancient language. No one needs to know Hebrew. And you know, I went to some Shabbat services. And I certainly wasn't-- being raised in a spiritual home, I was very open to all different ways of practicing. And I knew a lot about other religions. So I was open to it, but I definitely had a real resistance to it. And going to Poland and really-- leaving Israel, I kind of was thinking, oh, maybe I should stay and learn something about Judaism. And then after going to Poland and meeting the people who hid Oma and Opa, and, and seeing different camps, and, and doing that, by myself-- I really was alone for seven or eight days, there. Maybe it was longer-- I really realized, especially going in-- where was it? I think it was in Lublin, there's a-- it's a hospital now, but it used to be a yeshiva. And it was one of the largest yeshivas in Poland. And going in there, and there were-- I happened to be there during the March of the Living, not having any idea that that was going on then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were all these people davening in the front of, of this one room that had been preserved. And that, I just started crying. And I couldn't-- like, to see people worshipping, and praying, and speaking words of the Torah, and to me, it was such a powerful thing. I just felt like I needed to go and learn about this. And so I went back to Israel. And I got some scholarship money to go to some different learning programs, and then to a yeshiva in Jerusalem. And I stayed there for three months. And I was around a lot of very right-wing Jews, which was not politically where I was. But that feeling-- really being around people who really believed in the Torah and really practiced-- really practicing Judaism. And to be around these families-- I felt like, these are the healthiest families I've ever seen in my life. Like, the children listen to the parents. And the, and they-- seeing everyone come together for Shabbat, and I really felt Shabbat. And I really felt what it was to learn Torah, and to do something, to be doing rituals that my ancestors had done, knowing that both of my grandparents had come from families that practiced quite a bit, even though they both were very different. I really felt it wasn't an intellectual thing, at all. I just felt how strong the connection with my ancestors were. And that's something that's a very strong part of other traditions that I had been involved in in America. And that, to me, was just so overwhelming and clear. And I came back feeling like, I have to figure out how to bring my previous self and, and myself that wants to be learning more about Torah and, and being more of a practicing Jew together. So that's where I am now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know all this about your granddaughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I know a lot. I know a lot that she's looking for. And I tell her to go to synagogue, but she like better to go to Harvard and study. [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] No. No. I go to synagogue. And I very much what to be doing traditional learning. And she makes Shabbat. And, yeah. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that anybody else would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I do. I, Kendra, my youngest daughter, couldn't be here. And she faxed me a poem that she picked out with love and care that she wanted me to read. And I left it at home. So I'm really sorry. And I know that she's got a lot of feelings about this, so I'm sorry that she can't be here.

words.text: this, so I'm sorry that she

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to say something, too. I I know one reason that the kids didn't have a really Jewish-- because they went on Sunday to their father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my kids. [? SELMA ENGEL: ?] And. [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] Saturday and Sunday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their kids, yeah, [? Tagan ?] and Kendra. So I wanted them to go to [? Sunday ?] school. And we would have brought them, but it was very important that they go to the father, too, because they were divorced. So and that is. that is-- I know that was very upsetting for both of us--

speaker: interviewee
text: That could be part of it, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: That they didn't get a Jewish education, because she was too confused to do anything. She was looking for herself out. And so she couldn't do it. But there was no possibilities. And that little bit what we did in Judaism, when they came Friday evening, we made Shabbat, and, and things like that. But that all what we could give them.

speaker: interviewer
text: It seems like it was a lot. [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] Mm-hmm. Thank you all very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to make the [? family ?] pictures from [? Tagan? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] So this is [INAUDIBLE], who was the wife of the couple who hid my grandparents in Poland. And her husband died a year before these pictures were taken. This is her son and his wife, who, her son was a baby when Oma and Opa were staying with them. And these are her grandchildren. And this the-- somebody else. So this is me with their family in her son's house. And after her husband died, she moved into her son's house. So this is their old house, where they lived when my grandparents were hidden there. And there was a barn, right here, which is right, right here. That's the barn where they were hidden for nine months, in the hayloft of that barn. Do you know their full names, again? [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] What were their full names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] and Adam [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town was this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? TAGAN(GRANDDAUGHTER): ?] In [POLISH], in Poland. Should I-- oh, she's on the microphone. So after I left there, I went to [INAUDIBLE] or Lodz, which is-- there's a very large cemetery there, which I believe is the largest standing Jewish cemetery still today. And I had the information from the picture that my opa has, from he and his father and his brother at his mother's grave before they went to the camps. And with that information, I went to this man, who had blueprints of the whole cemetery and helped me to find this gravestone of [? Frieda ?] Engel. And it has the Shabbat candles, which are on the women's gravestones, and the broken tree, which shows that she died at a young age. And then this is the picture of me at her gravestone. And, and I regretted the fact-- I left the grave, and I forgot to put a stone on top of the grave. And I went, I couldn't find it when I went to go back, because it was such a huge cemetery. But I figured it was fine. So, that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ta
